intro:
The Fall of Man is the theological term for humanity’s primal act of rebellion against God, recorded in Genesis 3, when the first human pair disobeyed their Creator and so plunged the race into sin, guilt, and death. It marks the catastrophic departure from the original goodness in which humanity was made.

Created in God’s image and placed in Eden, Adam and Eve were given freedom with a single prohibition: not to eat of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, "for in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die" (Genesis 2:16–17). The serpent tempted them by casting doubt on God’s word and goodness and promising "you will be like God" (Genesis 3:1–6); they ate, grasping at autonomy from their Maker. At once came shame and hiding from God (Genesis 3:7–8).

The consequences reshaped the world: pain in childbearing, conflict, toil, and a cursed ground (Genesis 3:16–19); expulsion from the garden and the tree of life (Genesis 3:23–24); and death, returning to the dust. Yet even in judgment God gave the first promise of redemption — the protoevangelium — that the offspring of the woman would crush the serpent’s head (Genesis 3:15).

The New Testament reads the Fall as the hinge of human history. Through Adam "sin came into the world, and death through sin," spreading to all (Romans 5:12); by one man’s disobedience the many were made sinners (Romans 5:18–19), leaving humanity "by nature… dead in… trespasses" (Ephesians 2:1–3). Against this stands the second Adam: "as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive" (1 Corinthians 15:22; 1 Corinthians 15:45). The Fall thus explains the universal human condition — sin, death, and the marred image — and sets the stage for the whole drama of redemption, fulfilled when the woman’s offspring undoes the serpent’s work.
